executiveSummary.text: Diageo is a quality compounder trading at decade-low valuations following a significant operational reset. The February 2026 H1 results delivered a kitchen-sink moment: dividend halved, organic guidance cut to −2% to −3%, and a frank admission that volume recovery in the US and Asia will take longer than previously guided. Incoming CEO Sir Dave Lewis brings a credible turnaround template from his Tesco tenure, and the $2.3bn East Africa disposal at 17× EBITDA demonstrates management's willingnes

businessModel.text: Diageo is the world's largest premium spirits company by revenue. Its portfolio spans Scotch whisky (Johnnie Walker - the world's best-selling Scotch), Irish stout (Guinness), gin (Tanqueray, Gordon's), Irish cream (Baileys), and tequila (Casamigos, Don Julio). The group has ~200 brands sold in ~180 markets. Revenue is approximately 60% Americas and Europe , 40% Asia, Africa and emerging markets. The core strategy is premiumisation - pushing volume up the price ladder across whisky, tequila, and gin categories - supplemented by portfolio rationalisation via disposals of non-core or lower-margin assets. The economic moat is genuine: brand equity accumulated over decades, distribution locked in through on-trade relationships globally, and barriers to replicating aged spirit stocks at scale. The vulnerability is cyclicality - premium spirits are a discretionary purchase sensitive to consumer confidence, particularly in the key US market.

financialSnapshot.text: Revenue of approximately $20bn with free cash flow maintained at ~$3bn despite volume headwinds - evidence of the operating leverage in the model. Net debt of $13bn is the primary financial constraint, limiting buyback and M&A optionality. At 1,390.2p, Diageo trades on a forward P/E of 11.5× and EV/EBITDA of 10.5× - both near decade lows for the stock and at a meaningful discount to global spirits peers (LVMH Moët Hennessy, Brown-Forman, Ré[the analyst's] Cointreau). Post-cut dividend yield of ~4.2% provides some income support but the income floor is weakened by the cut. ROIC of 11.4% remains above WACC, confirming the business is still generating economic value - the problem is growth and sentiment, not the underlying economics.

recentCatalysts.text: H1 FY26 results (25 Feb 2026): Kitchen-sink reset. Organic net sales guidance cut to −2% to −3% for the full year. Dividend halved. Management explicitly acknowledged the US spirits category is recovering more slowly than forecast. New CEO - Sir Dave Lewis (in post 1 Jan 2026): Former CEO of Tesco, where he executed a successful multi-year turnaround. Strong track record of portfolio rationalisation, cost discipline, and credibility restoration with institutional shareholders. US spirits category weakness: The US represents ~35% of revenue. Post-pandemic normalisation has proven deeper and more persistent than Diageo or peers anticipated, with category volumes declining across premium spirits. Chinese baijiu decline: Diageo's Shui Jing Fang brand and broader exposure to Chinese white spirits has been a drag on Asia numbers. Consumer confidence in China remains soft, and the baijiu premium category has seen meaningful volume declines. $2.3bn East Africa disposal (H2 2026): Sale of the East Africa business at 17× EBITDA - above market expectations. Proceeds will partially delever the balance sheet and demonstrate management's commitment to portfolio optimisation.
items: H1 FY26 results (25 Feb 2026): Kitchen-sink reset. Organic net sales guidance cut to −2% to −3% for the full year. Dividend halved. Management explicitly acknowledged the US spirits category is recovering more slowly than forecast.

New CEO - Sir Dave Lewis (in post 1 Jan 2026): Former CEO of Tesco, where he executed a successful multi-year turnaround. Strong track record of portfolio rationalisation, cost discipline, and credibility restoration with institutional shareholders.

US spirits category weakness: The US represents ~35% of revenue. Post-pandemic normalisation has proven deeper and more persistent than Diageo or peers anticipated, with category volumes declining across premium spirits.

Chinese baijiu decline: Diageo's Shui Jing Fang brand and broader exposure to Chinese white spirits has been a drag on Asia numbers. Consumer confidence in China remains soft, and the baijiu premium category has seen meaningful volume declines.

$2.3bn East Africa disposal (H2 2026): Sale of the East Africa business at 17× EBITDA - above market expectations. Proceeds will partially delever the balance sheet and demonstrate management's commitment to portfolio optimisation.

thesisEvaluation.text: Scenario Probability Price Target Key Drivers Bull 25% 1,700p+ Lewis executes Tesco-style turnaround; US normalises H2 2026; sum-of-parts crystallised via disposals; re-rates to 15-16× EV/EBITDA Base 55% 1,380-1,500p Gradual US recovery; baijiu stabilises; FCF maintained; modest re-rating; dividend grows from reduced base Bear 20% 1,100-1,250p US spirits structural decline accelerates; China drag persists; debt constrains flexibility; income base permanently impaired Weighted conviction: 25% × 1,700 + 55% × 1,440 + 20% × 1,175 = 1,426p . Adjusted to 62/100 to reflect new CEO optionality - Lewis has demonstrated he can execute exactly this type of turnaround, and his incentive package is aligned with long-term shareholder value.

keyRisks.text: 1 US spirits structural decline - If the post-pandemic premium spirits volume decline is structural (secular shift toward beer, RTDs, or simply reduced alcohol consumption) rather than cyclical, the recovery thesis fails. 35% of revenue exposure makes this the single largest risk. 2 China baijiu drag - Chinese consumer confidence recovery is uncertain. Shui Jing Fang and broader baijiu exposure could continue to drag reported numbers and management attention. 3 Net debt $13bn - Limits M&A optionality and share buybacks. If FCF deteriorates below the $3bn run-rate, leverage metrics will deteriorate and credit rating risk emerges. 4 Dividend cut effect - Income investors who owned Diageo for its reliable, growing dividend have been structurally de-motivated. The income floor is gone; re-building yield credibility takes multiple years of execution. 5 Lewis turnaround pace - Tesco was a domestic UK business; Diageo is a 200-brand global operation across 180 markets. The turnaround may be slower and more complex than the Tesco precedent suggests, testing investor patience.

risks.risk: 1 US spirits structural decline - If the post-pandemic premium spirits volume decline is structural (secular shift toward beer, RTDs, or simply reduced alcohol consumption) rather than cyclical, the recovery thesis fails. 35% of revenue exposure makes this the single largest risk.
2 China baijiu drag - Chinese consumer confidence recovery is uncertain. Shui Jing Fang and broader baijiu exposure could continue to drag reported numbers and management attention.
3 Net debt $13bn - Limits M&A optionality and share buybacks. If FCF deteriorates below the $3bn run-rate, leverage metrics will deteriorate and credit rating risk emerges.

4 Dividend cut effect - Income investors who owned Diageo for its reliable, growing dividend have been structurally de-motivated. The income floor is gone; re-building yield credibility takes multiple years of execution.

5 Lewis turnaround pace - Tesco was a domestic UK business; Diageo is a 200-brand global operation across 180 markets. The turnaround may be slower and more complex than the Tesco precedent suggests, testing investor patience.

whoShouldOwn.text: Should own: Long-term value investors seeking quality defensive exposure at decade-low valuations. Those who believe in the premium spirits category over a 3-5 year horizon and trust the new CEO's turnaround track record. Investors comfortable sitting through a slow-burning recovery without near-term catalysts. Should avoid: Anyone needing near-term price catalysts, income from a growing dividend, or who bought Diageo for its previous reputation as a reliable dividend compounder. Short-term traders and momentum investors should also stay away - the stock is structurally out of favour and the re-rating requires genuine operational improvement, not narrative.

recommendation.text: Conviction score: 62/100 . This reflects a quality business at low valuations with a credible new CEO, constrained by two significant near-term headwinds (US volume, China) that prevent a higher-conviction BUY. HOLD at 1,390.2p. Do not add at current prices unless you have a long time horizon and high risk tolerance for the bear scenario. Preferred entry points: Add on a break below 1,300p - materialises further downside, improving risk/reward Add on confirmed US volume recovery (reported in monthly Nielsen/IWSR data) Wait for August 2026 full-year results - clearest catalyst for re-rating; Lewis's first full FY in charge

entryExit.text: Below 1,300p: Accumulation zone - improving risk/reward on further downside 1,300p-1,450p: Fair hold range at current fundamentals 1,450p-1,700p: Trim / reassess - valuation starts to reflect recovery scenarios 1,700p+: Take profit - requires US volume recovery to be genuine, not priced in August 2026 FY results: Primary re-rating catalyst - Lewis's first full year in charge The upside to analyst consensus (~1,950p) is ~40% from current price - the question is timing and whether that consensus needs to come down further first.
